title: Chapter 24 summary and quiz
summary_md:
## Chapter 24 Summary

This chapter covered inheritance, the mechanism that lets one class reuse and extend another without duplicating code.

### Introduction and basic mechanics (24.1, 24.2)

Inheritance models an "is-a" relationship. You write `class Derived : public Base` to inherit all non-private members of `Base`. The derived class automatically gets the base class's public and protected member variables and functions. Constructors, destructors, and assignment operators are **not** inherited.

### Construction and destruction order (24.3)

The base portion is constructed first, then the derived portion. Destruction happens in reverse. This guarantees inherited members are initialized before the derived constructor body runs, and still intact when the derived destructor executes.

### Constructors and initialization (24.4)

Since constructors are not inherited, the derived class must call a base constructor in its member initializer list. Omitting this call makes the compiler attempt the base default constructor; if none exists, compilation fails.

### Access specifiers and inheritance (24.5)

Public inheritance keeps public members public and protected members protected. Protected inheritance makes both protected. Private inheritance makes both private. In practice, public inheritance is used almost exclusively.

### Adding and overriding functionality (24.6, 24.7)

Derived classes can add new members and override inherited functions by defining a function with the same name and signature. Call the base version with `Base::functionName()` to extend rather than fully replace inherited behavior.

### Hiding inherited functionality (24.8)

You can hide a base function by overriding it with `= delete` or by moving it to a different access level with a `using` declaration. A `using Base::functionName` in a different access section changes that function's visibility in the derived class.

### Multiple inheritance (24.9)

C++ allows inheriting from multiple bases: `class D : public B1, public B2`. This introduces the diamond problem: when two bases share a common ancestor, the derived class gets duplicate ancestor members. Explicit scope resolution disambiguates, but multiple inheritance adds complexity and should be used sparingly.

exercises.title: Employee Hierarchy with Construction Order and Overrides
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Build a two-level hierarchy (`Employee` -> `Manager`) demonstrating construction/destruction order, base constructor delegation, and overriding an inherited function.

## Input
- Line 1: a string `name` (no spaces), an integer `age`, and a string `role` (no spaces).
- Line 2: an integer `teamSize`.

## Output
```
Employee constructed: <name> age <age>
Manager constructed: <role> team <teamSize>

<name> (age <age>), <role>, manages <teamSize>

Manager destroyed
Employee destroyed
```
The blank lines separate construction, description, and destruction phases.

## Requirements
1. `Employee`: private `std::string m_name`, `int m_age`. Constructor prints `Employee constructed: <name> age <age>`. Destructor prints `Employee destroyed`. Public `describe() const` returns `"<name> (age <age>)"`.
2. `Manager` publicly inherits `Employee`. Adds private `std::string m_role`, `int m_teamSize`. Constructor delegates to `Employee`, prints `Manager constructed: <role> team <teamSize>`. Destructor prints `Manager destroyed`. Overrides `describe()` returning `"<Employee::describe()>, <role>, manages <teamSize>"` by calling the base version.
3. In `main`, create a `Manager` in a scope block. Print blank line, `describe()`, blank line between construction and destruction.
4. You may include `<iostream>`, `<string>`, `<string_view>`.

test_cases.label: Sample -- typical manager
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Employee constructed: Alice age 35
Manager constructed: Engineering team 8

Alice (age 35), Engineering, manages 8

Manager destroyed
Employee destroyed

test_cases.label: Hidden -- single-person team
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Employee constructed: Bob age 28
Manager constructed: Sales team 1

Bob (age 28), Sales, manages 1

Manager destroyed
Employee destroyed

test_cases.label: Hidden -- large team
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Employee constructed: Zara age 60
Manager constructed: Research team 50

Zara (age 60), Research, manages 50

Manager destroyed
Employee destroyed

exercises.title: Protected Members, New Functionality, and Hiding setSpeed
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Build a `Vehicle` base class and a `RaceCar` derived class that uses protected members to add a `boost()` function and hides the inherited `setSpeed()` to prevent arbitrary speed changes.

## Input
- Line 1: a string `model` (no spaces), an integer `topSpeed`, an integer `currentSpeed`.
- Line 2: an integer `boostCount` (0 <= boostCount <= 20).

## Output
```
<model>: <currentSpeed>/<topSpeed> km/h
Boost 1: <speed> km/h
Boost 2: <speed> km/h
...
<model>: <finalSpeed>/<topSpeed> km/h
setSpeed is disabled for RaceCar
```
Each boost adds 30, capped at `topSpeed`. The last line is printed when `setSpeed(0)` is called on the `RaceCar`.

## Requirements
1. `Vehicle`: protected `std::string m_model`, `int m_topSpeed`, `int m_currentSpeed`. Constructor takes all three. Public `status() const` prints `"<model>: <currentSpeed>/<topSpeed> km/h"`. Public `setSpeed(int)` sets `m_currentSpeed`.
2. `RaceCar` publicly inherits `Vehicle`. Adds `void boost()` (new functionality) that adds 30 to speed, capped at top speed. Adds `int getSpeed() const`. Hides `setSpeed(int)` by overriding it to print `"setSpeed is disabled for RaceCar"` without changing speed.
3. In `main`, create a `RaceCar`, print status, apply boosts printing each, print status, call `setSpeed(0)`.
4. You may include `<iostream>`, `<string>`, `<string_view>`, `<algorithm>`.

test_cases.label: Sample -- three boosts with cap
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Ferrari: 150/300 km/h
Boost 1: 180 km/h
Boost 2: 210 km/h
Boost 3: 240 km/h
Ferrari: 240/300 km/h
setSpeed is disabled for RaceCar

test_cases.label: Hidden -- boost hits top speed cap
test_cases.expected_stdout:
GoKart: 50/80 km/h
Boost 1: 80 km/h
Boost 2: 80 km/h
GoKart: 80/80 km/h
setSpeed is disabled for RaceCar

test_cases.label: Hidden -- zero boosts
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Sedan: 0/200 km/h
Sedan: 0/200 km/h
setSpeed is disabled for RaceCar
